name: dothttp-req

metadata.name: dothttp-req
metadata.summary: DotHttp recommended tool for making http requests.

documentation:
# Inspiration

With the rise in usage of microservices, Making http requests is essential job of most devs. For these, there are multiple options like curl, insomnia postman. My Ideal choice is to use curl but problems with it is, having no history and no easy way to save and rerun. Postman solves that problem, but once user logs in(earlier it used to be optional, now its mandatory), it stores all request metadata(body/headers/urls/credentails) to their servers although it helps solve using it across multiple devices, backup but it could be potential security loop hole if they can access it. (now a days, postman desktop is super slow also).

This project aims to solve save&reuse requests, maintaining history and no sync in proprietery servers (although you can achive sync via commiting http files into git).

## GOAL

dothttp will provide simple, cleaner architecture for making http requests. It uses xtext (eclipse developed dsl) to build a custom dsl.


### Documentation

More information or docs can be cound at https://docs.dothttp.dev


---
Go through this example for better understanding. for babysteps click [here](#first-dothttprequest-and-more)
```http
# users.http

#!/usr/bin/env /home/prasanth/cedric05/dothttp/dist/dothttp-cli

# this is comment

// this is also a comment

/*
   this is multi line
   comment
*/

# http file can have multiple requests, name tag/annotation is used to identify
@name("fetch 100 users, skip first 50")

# makes are get request, with url `https://req.dothttp.dev/user`
GET https://req.dothttp.dev/user

# below is an header example
"Authorization": "Basic dXNlcm5hbWU6cGFzc3dvcmQ="

# below is how you set url params '?' --> signifies url quary param
? ("fetch", "100") #
? ("skip", "50")
? projection, name
? projection, org
? projection, location




# makes are post request, with url `https://req.dothttp.dev/user`
POST https://req.dothttp.dev/user

basicauth('username', 'password')
/*
   below defines payload for the post request.
   json --> signifies payload is json data
*/
json({
    "name": "{{name=adam}}", # name is templated, if spcified via env or property, it will be replaced
    "org": "dothttp",
    "location": "Hyderabad",
    # "interests": ["exploring", "listening to music"],
})



# makes put request, with url `https://req.dothttp.dev/user/1`
PUT https://req.dothttp.dev/post

# define headers in .dothttp.json with env
basicauth("{{username}}, "{{password}}")

# posts with urlencoded
data({
    "name": "Adam A",
    "org": "dothttp",
    "location": "Hyderabad",
    "interests": ["exploring", "listening to music"],
})

// or use below one
// data('name=Adam+A&org=dothttp&location=Hyderabad&interests=%5B%27exploring%27%2C+%27listening+to+music%27%5D')
```

## KICKSTART

### From pypi

```shell
pip install dothttp-req==0.0.10
```

### From source

```shell
git clone git@github.com:cedric05/dothttp.git
cd dothttp

python3 -m pip install pipenv
pipenv install
```

### python3.9

```shell
python3 -m dothttp examples/dothttpazure.http
```

### docker

```shell
docker build -t dothttp .
docker run -it --rm dothttp
```

### whalebrew

```shell
docker run -it --rm dothttp
```

## Features

1. easy and cleaner http syntax
1. variable substitution with property file
1. generates curl from http for easy sharing

```shell
docker build -t dothttp .
whalebrew install dothttp
dothttp examples/dothttpazure.http
```

## First DotHttpRequest and more

```get.http
GET "http://localhost:8000/get"
```

`dothttp get.http`  or `python -m dothttp get.http`

## Run

`dothttp simple.http`

prints

```json
{
  "args": {},
  "headers": {
    "Accept-Encoding": "identity",
    "Host": "httpbin.org",
    "User-Agent": "python-urllib3/1.26.3",
    "X-Amzn-Trace-Id": "Root=1-6022266a-20fb552e530ba3d90c75be6d"
  },
  "origin": "117.216.243.24",
  "url": "http://localhost:8000/get"
}
```

### POST request

```post.http
POST "http://localhost:8000/post"
```

```json
{
  "args": {},
  "data": "",
  "files": {},
  "form": {},
  "headers": {
    "Accept-Encoding": "identity",
    "Content-Length": "0",
    "Host": "httpbin.org",
    "User-Agent": "python-urllib3/1.26.3",
    "X-Amzn-Trace-Id": "Root=1-602228fa-3c3ed5213b6d8c2d2a223148"
  },
  "json": null,
  "origin": "117.216.243.24",
  "url": "http://localhost:8000/post"
}
```

similarly, other methods`GET, POST, OPTIONS, DELETE, CONNECT, PUT, HEAD, TRACE` support is available.

### Query

query params can be added to request by specifying
` query ( "key", "value")`
` ?  "key", "value"`
` ? "key": "value"`
` ? "key"= "value"`
all four are accepted. going with `query("hi", "hi2)` is more readable. `?"key"= "value"` is more concise

### Payload

user can specify payload by mentioning below four forms (for various scenarios).

- `data("ram")`

  user can also mention its `content-type` with
  `data("ram", "text/plain")`
- `data({"key": "value"})` for form input.
- `json({"key": "value"})` for json payload.
- `fileinput("path/to/file", "type")` uploads file as payload (type is optional).
- `files(("photo", "path/to/file/photo.jpg", "image/jpeg"),
  ("photo details", '{"name":"prasanth"}', "application/json")   
  )`

  for multipart upload
  **dothttp** will figure out content type by going through file/data, when type is not mentioned.

### Comments

**dothttp** will use `#` for commenting entire line.

1. `//` line comment. follows java, javascript
2. `#` line comment. follows python's comment style
3. `/*
   */` multi line comment. follows java/javascript style

### Templating

```.http
POST 'http://localhost:8000/post'
? ("{{key}}", "{{value}}")
data('{"{{key}}" :"{{value}}"}', 'application/json')
```

- specify variable values through property file ([sample.json](./examples/.dothttp.json)).
    - user can define environments and can activate multiple environments at a time
    - **dothttp** by default will read variables from `"*"` section
    - for example
      `dothttp --property-file path/to/file.json --env ram chandra`

      will activate `*` section properties, `ram` section properties and `chandra` section properties
      `dothttp --env ram chandra`
      will activate `*` section properties, `ram` section properties and `chandra` section properties
      from `.dothttp.json` in httpfile name space
- through command line
  `dothttp --property key=ram value=ranga`
  will replace `{{ram}}` to `ranga` from the file
- through file itself. (will be helpful for default properties)

```
POST 'https://{{host=httpbin.org}}/post'
```

### Headers

User can define headers in below three formats

1. `header('content-type', 'application/json')` readable
2. `'content-type': 'application/json'` concise
3. property file `headers` section from property-file can also be used. in most scenarios, headers section will be
   common for a host. having them in property file would ease them.

### Authentication

#### BasicAuth
`basicauth('username','password')'` --> will compute add respective headers.

#### DigestAuth
`digestauth('username','password')'` --> will compute add respective headers.

#### NtlmAuth
`ntlmauth('username','password')'` --> will compute add respective headers.

### Property file

```json
{
  "*": {
    "host": "httpbin.org"
  },
  "headers": {
    "content-type": "plain/text"
  },
  "preprod": {
    "host": "preprod.httpbin.org"
  }
}
```

#### Special sections in property file

1. `*` section in property file will be activated once user specifies property file if user didn't specifiy file
   and `.dothttp.json` exists, it will be activated
2. `headers` once a property file is activated. headers from property file will be added to request by default without
   user having to specify in `.http` file

#### Formatter (experimental phase)

**dothttp** can format a http file using below command
`dothttp -fmt examples/dothttpazure.http --experimental`
or
`dothttp --format examples/dothttpazure.http --experimental`

to print to command line

`dothttp --format examples/dothttpazure.http --experimental --stdout`

### Editor support

syntax highlighting for visual studio code is supported
via [dothttp-code](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=ShivaPrasanth.dothttp-code)

### Command line options

```
usage: dothttp [-h] [--curl] [--property-file PROPERTY_FILE] [--no-cookie] [--env ENV [ENV ...]] [--debug] [--info] [--format] [--stdout]
               [--property PROPERTY [PROPERTY ...]]
               file

http requests for humans

optional arguments:
  -h, --help            show this help message and exit

general:
  --curl                generates curl script
  --no-cookie, -nc      cookie storage is disabled
  --debug, -d           debug will enable logs and exceptions
  --info, -i            more information
  file                  http file

property:
  --property-file PROPERTY_FILE, -p PROPERTY_FILE
                        property file
  --env ENV [ENV ...], -e ENV [ENV ...]
                        environment to select in property file. properties will be enabled on FIFO
  --property PROPERTY [PROPERTY ...]
                        list of property's

format:
  --format, -fmt        formatter
  --stdout              print to commandline
```

checkout [examples]('./examples/dothttpazure.http')

-----------
### Vscode alternatives

- [rest-client](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=humao.rest-client) written in typescript
- [httpYac](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=anweber.vscode-httpyac)  written in typescript
- [Thunder Client](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=rangav.vscode-thunder-client) closed source

---------
### Non Vscode alternatives
- [http-client](https://www.jetbrains.com/help/idea/http-client-in-product-code-editor.html)  closed source
- [dothttp](https://github.com/tonsV2/dothttp)  written in python

functions.name: dothttp_req.json_utils.py_encode_basestring
documentation: Return a JSON representation of a Python string

methods.name: dothttp_req.json_utils.JSONEncoder.iterencode
documentation:
Encode the given object and yield each string
representation as available.

For example:

```
for chunk in JSONEncoder().iterencode(bigobject):
    mysocket.write(chunk)
```

classes.name: dothttp_req.property_util.PropertyProvider

1. properties defined in file itself ({{a=10}})

        {{ a="ranga "}}
        in above case whitespace is considered

    4. properties from files's '*'

methods.name: dothttp_req.BaseModelProcessor.load_properties_n_headers
dothttp_req.HttpDefBase.load_headers
documentation:
entrypoints
        1. dev defines headers in http file
        2. dev defines headers in property file
        3. dev defines headers via basic auth (only for Authorization)
        4. dev can define in data/file/files's type attribute section for ('content-type')
:return:

methods.name: dothttp_req.HttpDefBase._load_payload
documentation:
1. dev can define data with string
    2. dev can define data with json (will be sent as form)
    3. dev can define a file upload (will be sent as file upload)
    4. dev can define a json payload (will be sent as json string as payload)
    5. dev can define multipart
        5.1 dev can define file upload (header also optional)
        5.2 dev can define data input (header also, optional)
:return:

methods.name: dothttp.HttpDefBase._load_payload
documentation:
1. dev can define data with string
    2. dev can define data with json (will be sent as form)
    3. dev can define a file upload (will be sent as file upload)
    4. dev can define a json payload (will be sent as json string as payload)
    5. dev can define multipart
        5.1 dev can define file upload (header also optional)
        5.2 dev can define data input (header also, optional)
:return:

modules.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1
documentation: To use this code, make sure you
import json
and then, to convert JSON from a string, do
result = postman21_collection_from_dict(json.loads(json_string))

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.ApikeyElement
documentation:
Represents an attribute for any authorization method provided by Postman. For example
`username` and `password` are set as auth attributes for Basic Authentication method.
To use this code, make sure you
import json
and then, to convert JSON from a string, do
result = postman_collection21_from_dict(json.loads(json_string))

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Description

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Description.content
documentation:
Holds the mime type of the raw description content. E.g: 'text/markdown' or 'text/html'.
The type is used to correctly render the description when generating documentation, or in
the Postman app.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Description.type
documentation: Description can have versions associated with it, which should be put in this property.
The content of the description goes here, as a raw string.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.QueryParam.description
documentation: If set to true, the current query parameter will not be sent with the request.

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.VariableType
documentation: A variable may have multiple types. This field specifies the type of the variable.

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Variable

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Variable.disabled
documentation:
A variable ID is a unique user-defined value that identifies the variable within a
collection. In traditional terms, this would be a variable name.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Variable.id
documentation:
A variable key is a human friendly value that identifies the variable within a
collection. In traditional terms, this would be a variable name.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Variable.key
documentation: Variable name

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Variable.name
documentation: When set to true, indicates that this variable has been set by Postman

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Variable.system
documentation: A variable may have multiple types. This field specifies the type of the variable.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Variable.type
documentation:
The value that a variable holds in this collection. Ultimately, the variables will be
replaced by this value, when say running a set of requests from a collection

Collection variables allow you to define a set of variables, that are a *part of the
collection*, as opposed to environments, which are separate entities.
*Note: Collection variables must not contain any sensitive information.*

Using variables in your Postman requests eliminates the need to duplicate requests, which
can save a lot of time. Variables can be defined, and referenced to from any part of a
request.

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.URLClass

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.URLClass.hash
documentation:
The host for the URL, E.g: api.yourdomain.com. Can be stored as a string or as an array
of strings.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.URLClass.path
documentation:
The port number present in this URL. An empty value implies 80/443 depending on whether
the protocol field contains http/https.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.URLClass.port
documentation: The protocol associated with the request, E.g: 'http'

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.URLClass.protocol
documentation:
An array of QueryParams, which is basically the query string part of the URL, parsed into
separate variables

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.URLClass.query
documentation:
The string representation of the request URL, including the protocol, host, path, hash,
query parameter(s) and path variable(s).

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.URLClass.raw
documentation:
Postman supports path variables with the syntax `/path/:variableName/to/somewhere`. These
variables are stored in this field.

Contains the URL fragment (if any). Usually this is not transmitted over the network, but
it could be useful to store this in some cases.

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Script

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Script.exec
documentation: A unique, user defined identifier that can  be used to refer to this script from requests.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Script.id
documentation: Script name

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Script.src
documentation: Type of the script. E.g: 'text/javascript'

A script is a snippet of Javascript code that can be used to to perform setup or teardown
operations on a particular response.

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Event

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Event.disabled
documentation: A unique identifier for the enclosing event.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Event.id
documentation: Can be set to `test` or `prerequest` for test scripts or pre-request scripts respectively.

Postman allows you to configure scripts to run when specific events occur. These scripts
are stored here, and can be referenced in the collection by their ID.

Defines a script associated with an associated event name

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.CollectionVersionClass

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.CollectionVersionClass.identifier
documentation:
Increment this number if you make changes to the collection that changes its behaviour.
E.g: Removing or adding new test scripts. (partly or completely).

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.CollectionVersionClass.meta
documentation:
You should increment this number if you make changes that will not break anything that
uses the collection. E.g: removing a folder.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.CollectionVersionClass.minor
documentation: Ideally, minor changes to a collection should result in the increment of this number.
A human friendly identifier to make sense of the version numbers. E.g: 'beta-3'

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Information

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Information.description
documentation:
A collection's friendly name is defined by this field. You would want to set this field
to a value that would allow you to easily identify this collection among a bunch of other
collections, as such outlining its usage or content.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Information.name
documentation:
This should ideally hold a link to the Postman schema that is used to validate this
collection. E.g: https://schema.getpostman.com/collection/v1
Detailed description of the info block

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.FormParameter

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.FormParameter.description
documentation: When set to true, prevents this form data entity from being sent.
Override Content-Type header of this form data entity.

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Mode
documentation: Postman stores the type of data associated with this request in this field.

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Body

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Body.graphql
documentation: Postman stores the type of data associated with this request in this field.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Body.mode
documentation: Additional configurations and options set for various body modes.
This field contains the data usually contained in the request body.

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.CERT
documentation: An object containing path to file certificate, on the file system

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Key
documentation: An object containing path to file containing private key, on the file system

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Certificate

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Cookie.domain
documentation: When the cookie expires.

documentation:
Custom attributes for a cookie go here, such as the [Priority
Field](https://code.google.com/p/chromium/issues/detail?id=232693)

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Cookie.extensions
documentation:
True if the cookie is a host-only cookie. (i.e. a request's URL domain must exactly match
the domain of the cookie).

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Cookie.host_only
documentation:
Indicates if this cookie is HTTP Only. (if True, the cookie is inaccessible to
client-side scripts)

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Cookie.max_age
documentation: This is the name of the Cookie.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Cookie.name
documentation: The path associated with the Cookie.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Cookie.path
documentation:
Indicates if the 'secure' flag is set on the Cookie, meaning that it is transmitted over
secure connections only. (typically HTTPS)

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Cookie.secure
documentation: True if the cookie is a session cookie.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Cookie.session
documentation: The value of the Cookie.

A Cookie, that follows the [Google Chrome
format](https://developer.chrome.com/extensions/cookies)

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.ResponseClass.body
documentation: The numerical response code, example: 200, 201, 404, etc.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.ResponseClass.header
documentation:
A unique, user defined identifier that can  be used to refer to this response from
requests.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.ResponseClass.original_request
documentation:
The time taken by the request to complete. If a number, the unit is milliseconds. If the
response is manually created, this can be set to `null`.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.ResponseClass.response_time
documentation: The response status, e.g: '200 OK'

documentation: Set of timing information related to request and response in milliseconds
The raw text of the response.

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Items

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Items.event
documentation: A unique ID that is used to identify collections internally

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Items.id
documentation:
A human readable identifier for the current item.

A folder's friendly name is defined by this field. You would want to set this field to a
value that would allow you to easily identify this folder.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.Items.auth
documentation:
Items are entities which contain an actual HTTP request, and sample responses attached to
it. Folders may contain many items.

Items are entities which contain an actual HTTP request, and sample responses attached to
it.

One of the primary goals of Postman is to organize the development of APIs. To this end,
it is necessary to be able to group requests together. This can be achived using
'Folders'. A folder just is an ordered set of requests.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman2_1.PostmanCollection21.info
documentation:
Items are the basic unit for a Postman collection. You can think of them as corresponding
to a single API endpoint. Each Item has one request and may have multiple API responses
associated with it.

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Auth

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Auth.apikey
documentation:
The attributes for [AWS
Auth](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/dev/RESTAuthentication.html). e.g.
accessKey, secretKey, region, service.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Auth.awsv4
documentation:
The attributes for [Basic
Authentication](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Basic_access_authentication). e.g.
username, password.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Auth.basic
documentation:
The attributes for [Bearer Token Authentication](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6750).
e.g. token.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Auth.bearer
documentation:
The attributes for [Digest
Authentication](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Digest_access_authentication). e.g.
username, password, realm, nonce, nonceCount, algorithm, qop, opaque, clientNonce.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Auth.digest
documentation:
The attributes for [Akamai EdgeGrid
Authentication](https://developer.akamai.com/legacy/introduction/Client_Auth.html). e.g.
accessToken, clientToken, clientSecret, baseURL, nonce, timestamp, headersToSign.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Auth.edgegrid
documentation:
The attributes for [Hawk Authentication](https://github.com/hueniverse/hawk). e.g.
authId, authKey, algorith, user, nonce, extraData, appId, delegation, timestamp.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Auth.noauth
documentation:
The attributes for [NTLM
Authentication](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/cc237488.aspx). e.g. username,
password, domain, workstation.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Auth.ntlm
documentation:
The attributes for [OAuth1](https://oauth.net/1/). e.g. consumerKey, consumerSecret,
token, tokenSecret, signatureMethod, timestamp, nonce, version, realm, encodeOAuthSign.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Auth.oauth1
documentation: The attributes for [OAuth2](https://oauth.net/2/). e.g. accessToken, addTokenTo.
Represents authentication helpers provided by Postman

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Description

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Description.content
documentation:
Holds the mime type of the raw description content. E.g: 'text/markdown' or 'text/html'.
The type is used to correctly render the description when generating documentation, or in
the Postman app.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Description.type
documentation: Description can have versions associated with it, which should be put in this property.
The content of the description goes here, as a raw string.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.QueryParam.description
documentation: If set to true, the current query parameter will not be sent with the request.

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman.VariableType
documentation: A variable may have multiple types. This field specifies the type of the variable.

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Variable

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Variable.disabled
documentation:
A variable ID is a unique user-defined value that identifies the variable within a
collection. In traditional terms, this would be a variable name.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Variable.id
documentation:
A variable key is a human friendly value that identifies the variable within a
collection. In traditional terms, this would be a variable name.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Variable.key
documentation: Variable name

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Variable.name
documentation: When set to true, indicates that this variable has been set by Postman

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Variable.system
documentation: A variable may have multiple types. This field specifies the type of the variable.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Variable.type
documentation:
The value that a variable holds in this collection. Ultimately, the variables will be
replaced by this value, when say running a set of requests from a collection

Collection variables allow you to define a set of variables, that are a *part of the
collection*, as opposed to environments, which are separate entities.
*Note: Collection variables must not contain any sensitive information.*

Using variables in your Postman requests eliminates the need to duplicate requests, which
can save a lot of time. Variables can be defined, and referenced to from any part of a
request.

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman.URLClass

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.URLClass.hash
documentation:
The host for the URL, E.g: api.yourdomain.com. Can be stored as a string or as an array
of strings.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.URLClass.path
documentation:
The port number present in this URL. An empty value implies 80/443 depending on whether
the protocol field contains http/https.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.URLClass.port
documentation: The protocol associated with the request, E.g: 'http'

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.URLClass.protocol
documentation:
An array of QueryParams, which is basically the query string part of the URL, parsed into
separate variables

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.URLClass.query
documentation:
The string representation of the request URL, including the protocol, host, path, hash,
query parameter(s) and path variable(s).

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.URLClass.raw
documentation:
Postman supports path variables with the syntax `/path/:variableName/to/somewhere`. These
variables are stored in this field.

Contains the URL fragment (if any). Usually this is not transmitted over the network, but
it could be useful to store this in some cases.

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Script

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Script.exec
documentation: A unique, user defined identifier that can  be used to refer to this script from requests.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Script.id
documentation: Script name

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Script.src
documentation: Type of the script. E.g: 'text/javascript'

A script is a snippet of Javascript code that can be used to to perform setup or teardown
operations on a particular response.

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Event

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Event.disabled
documentation: A unique identifier for the enclosing event.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Event.id
documentation: Can be set to `test` or `prerequest` for test scripts or pre-request scripts respectively.

Postman allows you to configure scripts to run when specific events occur. These scripts
are stored here, and can be referenced in the collection by their ID.

Defines a script associated with an associated event name

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman.CollectionVersionClass

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.CollectionVersionClass.identifier
documentation:
Increment this number if you make changes to the collection that changes its behaviour.
E.g: Removing or adding new test scripts. (partly or completely).

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.CollectionVersionClass.meta
documentation:
You should increment this number if you make changes that will not break anything that
uses the collection. E.g: removing a folder.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.CollectionVersionClass.minor
documentation: Ideally, minor changes to a collection should result in the increment of this number.
A human friendly identifier to make sense of the version numbers. E.g: 'beta-3'

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Information

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Information.description
documentation:
A collection's friendly name is defined by this field. You would want to set this field
to a value that would allow you to easily identify this collection among a bunch of other
collections, as such outlining its usage or content.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Information.name
documentation:
This should ideally hold a link to the Postman schema that is used to validate this
collection. E.g: https://schema.getpostman.com/collection/v1
Detailed description of the info block

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman.FormParameter

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.FormParameter.description
documentation: When set to true, prevents this form data entity from being sent.
Override Content-Type header of this form data entity.

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Mode
documentation: Postman stores the type of data associated with this request in this field.

classes.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Body

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Body.graphql
documentation: Postman stores the type of data associated with this request in this field.

class_variables.name: dotextensions.server.postman.Body.mode
documentation: Additional configurations and options set for various body modes.
This field contains the data usually contained in the request body.

documentation:
